rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe proximal myopathy driven by a recently doubled high-dose statin plus an interacting macrolide antibiotic carries a real risk of rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury. That risk CANNOT be excluded by telehealth: it needs creatine kinase and renal-function bloods plus hands-on assessment. Vitals are reassuring and there is no gross myoglobinuria yet, so this is not an immediate 000 ambulance emergency (T5); but it requires urgent SAME-DAY in-person assessment (T3) with bloods, alongside immediate cessation of the offending drugs. Under-triage to routine or 24-48h GP review would risk missing evolving rhabdomyolysis/AKI overnight; the frail patient's loss of mobility and falls risk reinforce same-day review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Dark, brown, tea- or cola-coloured urine, or a marked reduction in urine passed
escalation_edges.rationale: Myoglobinuria or falling urine output signals rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury and needs emergency-department assessment and IV fluids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your urine turns dark brown or tea-coloured, or you're passing much less than usual, go straight to an emergency department now.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Weakness getting much worse or spreading, new difficulty breathing or swallowing, or a fall/inability to move
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: same day / immediately if a fall or breathing difficulty
escalation_edges.rationale: Progressive weakness suggests worsening muscle injury; involvement of breathing/swallowing muscles or a fall is an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the weakness spreads or gets much worse, you have trouble breathing or swallowing, or you have a fall, get emergency help straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe vomiting, confusion, chest pain, palpitations, or collapse
escalation_edges.rationale: Vomiting/confusion/collapse can reflect severe electrolyte disturbance or renal failure from rhabdomyolysis — a medical emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start vomiting a lot, become confused, get chest pain or palpitations, or collapse, call 000 for an ambulance.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Same-day GP appointment and pathology are not available (e.g. after hours)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: same day — do not wait until the next day
escalation_edges.rationale: The CK and renal bloods are time-critical; if same-day GP/pathology cannot be arranged, urgent care or an emergency department should perform them rather than deferring.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't be seen by a GP with blood tests today, go to an urgent care clinic or emergency department for the tests — don't wait until tomorrow.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail older adult with abrupt loss of independent mobility and high falls risk (unable to transfer without help; very low, unsafe sofa)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to escalate and ensure the patient is not left unsupervised; arrange safe seating (firm high-backed chair with armrests) and assistance with transfers while awaiting review.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse is performing all transfers unaided
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the carer can safely supervise until the same-day review; provide the falls-prevention and red-flag advice to the spouse as well as the patient.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Stop the cholesterol tablet now and hold the antibiotic, and arrange to be seen in person TODAY for urgent blood tests — a muscle enzyme called CK and your kidney function. In the meantime have someone help you move and use a firm, high chair with armrests to avoid a fall.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Contact your GP for an urgent same-day appointment and same-day pathology; use an after-hours or urgent-care service if your usual GP is closed; call 000 or go to an emergency department if red-flag symptoms appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and you cannot get a same-day GP appointment with blood tests, go to an urgent care clinic or emergency department for the tests — do not wait until the next day.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring all your medication boxes. Tell the clinician the cholesterol tablet dose was doubled about a month ago and that you recently started an antibiotic, and that you have been taking them together. Ask for CK, U&E/eGFR and liver function tests.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating severe proximal myopathy with a statin-macrolide interaction as benign muscle pain and deferring to self-care or routine/48-hour GP review without same-day bloods or drug cessation.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised rising CK progresses to rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury over the following day; the offending drugs continue to be taken together.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranging same-day in-person review but failing to also advise immediate cessation of the statin/interacting antibiotic or to specify CK and renal-function bloods and rhabdomyolysis safety-netting.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a haemodynamically stable patient with no red-flag features, when urgent same-day in-person assessment with bloods is the appropriate disposition.
